content.text: Why Mitigation Must Be Resolved Before Build Starts

The most expensive time to discover that a risk cannot be mitigated is during user acceptance testing, after three sprints of engineering work. The second most expensive time is in production. The cheapest time is before a single line of implementation code is written.

A mitigation plan is not a promise to think about a risk later. It is a concrete specification of what will be built, how it will be verified, and what the acceptance threshold is — written before the build sprint begins.

This sub-subtopic covers the four mitigation strategies for Claude features, how to select the right strategy per risk, how to write a mitigation specification that engineering can act on, and how to verify that a mitigation is working after it is implemented.
Mitigation Strategy Selection

attrs.alt: Mitigation strategy chart: cost vs risk reduction — prompt constraints and must-not-do eval gates offer the best ROI; human-in-the-loop offers high reduction at high cost; rollback plan is last resort

content.text: Not all mitigations are equal. Choose the highest-reduction strategy that fits the risk severity. Always try the low-cost strategies first — prompt constraints and eval gates — before committing to expensive controls like human review loops.
The Four Mitigation Strategies
Strategy 1: Prompt-Level Constraints

The simplest and most effective mitigation for output quality risks is adding an explicit constraint to the system prompt. This works because the model reads and respects constraints when they are unambiguous, complete, and positively stated.
Use for
: hallucination of specific claim types, scope drift, tone deviation, format non-compliance.
Verification
: run the must-not-do eval suite; the pass rate for the targeted behaviour must reach the threshold.
Limitation
: only works for behaviours the model can actually follow from text instruction. Does not work for failures caused by model capability limits.
Strategy 2: Tool-Use Schema Validation

When a Claude feature uses tools (function calling), the schema itself is a mitigation. A tight schema with enum fields and required properties prevents the model from producing outputs that violate type or domain constraints.
Use for
: hallucination of specific field values, invalid state transitions, format non-compliance in structured outputs.
Verification
: schema validation is mechanical — the output either conforms to the schema or it does not. Use Pydantic or JSON Schema validation in the eval runner.
Limitation
: only applies to tool-use outputs. Does not constrain free-text outputs in the final message.
Strategy 3: Confidence Gating and Human Review
For high-stakes decisions, add an intermediate layer that holds the model output until a confidence threshold is met, or routes low-confidence outputs to human review.
Use for
: medical advice, legal interpretation, high-value financial recommendations, any domain where an incorrect model output has irreversible consequences.
Verification
: measure escalation rate in eval; it should match the rate calibrated during prototype testing.
Limitation
: requires a human review queue, which adds operational cost and latency. Size the queue before launch.
Strategy 4: Canary Rollout and Rollback Plan
For risks that cannot be fully mitigated before launch — residual operational risks, unknown interaction effects at scale — a staged rollout limits blast radius while production data is collected.
Use for
: latency risks at scale, cost overrun risks, unknown interaction effects with real user behaviour.
Verification
: define the metrics and alert thresholds before the canary begins. The rollout moves to the next stage only if those thresholds are met.
Limitation
: canary rollouts only work for risks that are observable in production data. Harm that is not logged cannot be detected in a canary.

# Canary rollout plan for Risk RISK-003 (Cost spike at peak traffic)
# Written before build start; executed by release engineer

rollout:
  feature: customer-support-escalation-agent
  risk_id: RISK-003
  risk_description: Token cost overrun at peak traffic

  stages:
    - name: canary-1pct
      traffic_pct: 1
      duration_hours: 24
      pass_criteria:
        avg_tokens_per_session: "<= 5000"
        p99_latency_ms: "<= 3500"
        error_rate_pct: "<= 0.5"
      actions_on_pass: advance_to_stage_2
      actions_on_fail: rollback_immediately

    - name: canary-10pct
      traffic_pct: 10
      duration_hours: 48
      pass_criteria:
        avg_tokens_per_session: "<= 5000"
        p99_latency_ms: "<= 3500"
        cost_per_thousand_sessions_usd: "<= 45"
        error_rate_pct: "<= 0.5"
      actions_on_pass: advance_to_stage_3
      actions_on_fail: rollback_immediately

    - name: full-rollout
      traffic_pct: 100
      duration_hours: continuous
      pass_criteria:
        avg_tokens_per_session: "<= 5500"
        p99_latency_ms: "<= 4000"
      actions_on_pass: mark_release_complete
      actions_on_fail: rollback_to_canary_10pct

  rollback:
    procedure: |
      1. Switch traffic back to previous prompt version via feature flag
      2. Alert engineering lead and PM
      3. Open incident with the stage metrics that triggered rollback
      4. Disable canary-stage feature flag
    rto_minutes: 5
    tested: false  # must be set to true before canary-1pct begins
Writing the Mitigation Specification

A mitigation plan that says 'we will add prompt constraints' is not a specification. Engineering needs to know exactly what will be built. Write the mitigation spec before the sprint backlog is finalised.
Field
What to specify
Example
Risk ID
The register entry this mitigates
RISK-001
Strategy
Which of the four strategies
Tool-use schema validation
Implementation
Exact change to code or prompt
Add enum constraint to `urgency` field; min-length 10 on `reason`
Verification method
How success is measured
Pydantic validation passes on all 200 eval cases
Success threshold
Quantitative pass criterion
Zero schema validation failures in eval suite
Fallback
What if the mitigation fails verification?
Escalate to human review on any validation failure
Owner
Named individual responsible for implementation
James Kim (Engineering)
Due date
Before build sprint starts or within sprint?
By end of sprint 1, day 3
Generating Mitigation Specs from the Risk Register
Verifying Mitigations Before Marking Risks Mitigated
A mitigation is not done when the code is written — it is done when the verification eval confirms the risk has been reduced to the residual level specified in the register.
Summary

Risk mitigation plans before build start translate risk register entries into concrete implementation specifications: what will be built, how it will be verified, what the success threshold is, and who is responsible. The four mitigation strategies for Claude features are prompt-level constraints (cheapest, highest ROI for output quality risks), tool-use schema validation (mechanical, effective for structured output risks), confidence gating with human review (high reduction for high-stakes decisions, high operational cost), and canary rollout with rollback plan (for residual operational risks that cannot be fully mitigated before launch). A mitigation is not complete until the verification eval confirms the residual risk has reached the level specified in the register. Writing mitigation specs before the build sprint begins is the intervention that most consistently prevents rework.
